lesson_title: Constructors and initialization of derived classes

cards.text:
To call a specific base class constructor from a derived class constructor, use:
{{c1::Derived(params) : Base{params} { }}}
cards.source: 24.4 - Constructors and initialization of derived classes

cards.front: Can a derived class initialize inherited member variables in its member initializer list?
cards.back: No, C++ prevents this. A member variable can only be initialized in a member initializer list of a constructor belonging to the same class as the variable.
cards.source: 24.4 - Constructors and initialization of derived classes

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Cannot initialize m_id in Derived's initializer list
cards.explanation: m_id is inherited from Base and can only be initialized in Base's constructor. Derived must call Base's constructor instead: Derived(double cost, int id) : Base{id}, m_cost{cost} { }
cards.source: 24.4 - Constructors and initialization of derived classes

cards.front: Why does C++ restrict initialization of inherited variables to the base class constructor?
cards.back: To ensure const and reference variables are initialized only once. If derived classes could re-initialize them, their values could be changed after being set, violating const correctness.
cards.source: 24.4 - Constructors and initialization of derived classes

cards.text: When calling a base constructor from a derived constructor's member initializer list, it {{c1::always}} executes {{c2::first}}, regardless of where it appears in the initializer list.
cards.source: 24.4 - Constructors and initialization of derived classes

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: C calls B, B calls A. A constructs first (prints 5), then B (prints 4.3), then C (prints 'R').
cards.source: 24.4 - Constructors and initialization of derived classes

cards.front: Can a constructor call its grandparent class constructor directly?
cards.back: No, constructors can only call constructors from their immediate parent/base class. The C constructor can only call B, not A directly.
cards.source: 24.4 - Constructors and initialization of derived classes

cards.text: Destructors are called in {{c1::reverse}} order of construction: from {{c2::most-derived}} to {{c2::most-base}}.
cards.source: 24.4 - Constructors and initialization of derived classes

cards.concept: Steps when instantiating a derived class
cards.explanation:
1. Memory for derived is allocated
2. Derived constructor is called
3. Compiler calls appropriate Base constructor
4. Base constructor initializer list runs
5. Base constructor body executes
6. Derived constructor initializer list runs
7. Derived constructor body executes
examples: Derived d{1.3, 5}; → Base(5) runs first, then Derived(1.3)
cards.source: 24.4 - Constructors and initialization of derived classes

cards.text: Derived classes can NOT access {{c1::private}} members of the base class directly. They must use {{c2::access functions}}.
cards.source: 24.4 - Constructors and initialization of derived classes

cards.front: Should you make base class member variables public for simplicity in derived classes?
cards.back: No, keep them private and use access functions. This maintains encapsulation and allows the base class to control how its data is accessed.
cards.source: 24.4 - Constructors and initialization of derived classes

cards.question: How would you fix this code to initialize both name and battingAverage?
cards.answer: Add name and age parameters, then call Person constructor
cards.explanation: BaseballPlayer(std::string_view name, int age, double avg) : Person{name, age}, m_battingAverage{avg} { }
cards.source: 24.4 - Constructors and initialization of derived classes

cards.front: If your base class has virtual functions, what should you do with the destructor?
cards.back: Make the destructor virtual as well, otherwise undefined behavior will result in certain cases.
cards.source: 24.4 - Constructors and initialization of derived classes
